coredata.dc:title: A facile and high-efficient approach to yellow emissive graphene quantum dots from graphene oxide
coredata.dc:creator: Zhao, Yan
Wu, Xiaolong
Sun, Shan
Ma, Liling
Zhang, Ling
Lin, Hengwei
coredata.dc:description: A facile and high-efficient approach for the preparation of graphene quantum dots (GQDs) is reported. With the assistance of KO2 in the hydrothermal cutting graphene oxide (GO), a conversion rate of ∼35 wt% from GO to GQDs is achieved. The as-prepared GQDs display rarely observed yellow emissive photoluminescence with a respectable quantum yield of 8.9% in H2O. Moreover, the GQDs are further demonstrated to perform as a potential pH probe and bioimaging agent.

xocs:funding-list.xocs:funding-text: This work was supported by the Natural Science Foundation of China ( 21277149 ), Zhejiang Provincial Natural Science Foundation of China ( LR13B050001 ), and the Ningbo Science and Technology Bureau ( 2014B82010 ). Appendix A

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: (a) TEM image of the GQDs. (b) TEM histogram of particle size distribution of the GQDs. (c) HR-TEM image of the GQDs. (d) AFM image and height profile of GQDs along the line from A to B. (A colour version of this figure can be viewed online.)
(a) XPS survey spectra of the GO and GQDs. XPS C1s analysis of the GO (b), and the GQDs (c). (d) FT-IR spectra of the GO and GQDs. (A colour version of this figure can be viewed online.)

(a) PL spectra of the GQDs (50 μM) in water. The inset shows a photograph of the GQDs solution under 365 nm UV light irradiation. (b) UV–vis absorption (black line) and excitation spectra (red line) of GQDs. (c) Fluorescence decays and fitting curve of the GQDs in water. (d) Relationship between PL intensity of the GQDs at 550 nm with pH (2−12). The inset indicates a linear fitting of PL intensity of the GQDs at 550 nm and pH from 2 to 9. (A colour version of this figure can be viewed online.)
Schematic illustrations of the hydrothermal cutting procedure for GO with the assistance of KO2. (A colour version of this figure can be viewed online.)

(a) Cellular cytotoxicity assessment of the as-obtained GQDs. (b)–(d) Confocal fluorescence images of MCF-7 cells incubated with the GQDs, (b) bright-field, (c) fluorescent field, and (d) overlayed images. (A colour version of this figure can be viewed online.)

head.ce:title: A facile and high-efficient approach to yellow emissive graphene quantum dots from graphene oxide

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: School of Chemistry and Environmental Engineering, Changchun University of Science and Technology, Changchun 130022, PR China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: School of Chemistry and Environmental Engineering
Changchun University of Science and Technology

ce:affiliation.ce:label: b
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Key Laboratory of Graphene Technologies and Applications of Zhejiang Province, Ningbo Institute of Materials Technology and Engineering (NIMTE), Chinese Academy of Sciences, Ningbo 315201, PR China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Key Laboratory of Graphene Technologies and Applications of Zhejiang Province
Ningbo Institute of Materials Technology and Engineering (NIMTE)

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: A facile and high-efficient approach for the preparation of graphene quantum dots (GQDs) is reported. With the assistance of KO2 in the hydrothermal cutting graphene oxide (GO), a conversion rate of ∼35 wt% from GO to GQDs is achieved. The as-prepared GQDs display rarely observed yellow emissive photoluminescence with a respectable quantum yield of 8.9% in H2O. Moreover, the GQDs are further demonstrated to perform as a potential pH probe and bioimaging agent.

ce:section.ce:para: Since the discovery of graphene in 2004 [1], this new material has attracted great scientific interests because of its outstanding physico-chemical properties and many potential applications. Pristine graphene is a zero band gap material and displays excellent electrical conductivity [2]. However, when the dimension of graphene is reduced to nanoscale, its band gap becomes discrete and photoluminescence (PL) could thus be observed due to the quantum confinement and edge effects [3–5]. Graphene quantum dots (GQDs), which are graphene nanosheets of usually less than 10 nm in size, exhibit unique optical and electronic properties and thus have been proposed to be applied in photovoltaic and optoelectronic devices [6–10]. Moreover, GQDs also show great potentials in bio-imaging and chemo-/bio-sensing due to their excellent biocompatibility and easy of functionalization [11–14].

Owing to the superior optoelectronic properties and excellent biocompatibility, GQDs have attracted growing interests for widely applications in recent years. Meanwhile, great efforts have also been devoted to develop methods for the preparation of GQDs, expecting with high PL quantum yields (QYs) and tunable emission wavelengths. To date, top-down approaches have been widely employed in the synthesis of GQDs through cutting carbon precursors into tiny dots by physical or chemical methods, ranging from nanocarbons (e.g. graphene sheet [15], graphene oxide (GO) [16,17], carbon nanotube [18], and fullerene [19]) to bulk graphite [20–22], carbon fiber [23] and coal [24]. However, most of the obtained GQDs displayed intense emission only at blue to green light regions, and the long wavelength (e.g. yellow to red light) emission was rarely observed. In addition, all the reported top-down methods for GQDs suffered from very low cutting efficiency (generally <5 wt% of the precursors) and low QYs, and/or requiring complicated synthetic procedures. For example, Pan et al. developed a hydrothermal route to cut GO sheets into blue PL GQDs with a conversion rate of only ca. 5 wt% [15]. Sun et al. reported an efficient and scalable synthesis of GQDs through directly oxidizing and etching graphite powder [21]. The reaction yield of their method was claimed as high as 70 wt%, but the extremely low QY (∼1%) of the obtained GQDs limited their further applications. Yang et al. reported a mild approach of one-step solvothermal method cutting GO in DMF [17]. The QY of their GQDs was enhanced to 11.4%, while their approach suffered from extremely low reaction yield of only 1.6 wt%. Later, a simple method for GQDs by extracting aromatic domains from GO in ammonia solution was reported [25]. Although these GQDs exhibiting relatively high QYs (19–29%) and tunable PL emissions (420–535 nm), yet this method meet the problem of low reaction yields as well.

To drive GQDs into practical applications, an effective and reproductive method for the synthesis of high quality products is still greatly desirable, but such a task has not yet been achieved. Considering the inert nature of carbon precursors, powerful “scissors” are required to effectively cut the carbon precursors, but meanwhile, the powerful “scissors” could as well as destroy the graphitic structure and produce more defects in GQDs, consequently reducing PL QYs of the obtained GQDs. Therefore, an appropriate “scissors” should be carefully screened for the preparation of high quality GQDs with superior PL properties. Herein, a very facile and high-efficient approach for the preparation of GQDs is reported using the commonly hydrothermal process with assistance of potassium superoxide (KO2) as a proper “scissors” to cut GO. The as-developed method was determined to be high conversion rate (∼35 wt% from GO), and the as-prepared GQDs exhibit rarely reported yellow emissive PL property and respectable QYs (8.9%) in aqueous media. Moreover, the GQDs are observed to be pH-dependent emission (linearly increasing from pH 2–9), low cytotoxicity and excellent cellular staining capability, demonstrating their potential applications as a pH probe and bio-imaging agent.

Natural graphite powder (200 meshes) was purchased from Alfa Aesar, Sodium nitrate and KO2 were bought from Aladdin Chemicals Co. Ltd (Shanghai, China). Potassium permanganate, sulfuric acid, ammonia water, sodium hydrate barium chloride were purchased from Sinopharm Chemical Reagent Co. Ltd (Shanghai, China). Deionized (DI) water was used throughout the experiments. All reagents were used as received without any further purification. Transmission electron microscopy (TEM) observations were performed on a Tecnai F20 microscope. Atomic force microscope (AFM) measurements were carried out with Veeco Dimension 3100 V. Fourier transform infrared (FT-IR) spectra were obtained on a Nicolet 6700 FT-IR spectrometer. XPS were carried out with ESCALAB 250Xi (Thermo Scientific). UV–vis absorption spectra were recorded on a PERSEE T10CS UV–vis spectrophotometer. PL emission and excitation spectra were measured on a Hitachi F-4600 spectrophotometer at ambient conditions. PL lifetime was measured using Fluorolog 3–11 (HORIBA Jobin Yvon). The absolute fluorescence quantum yield was determined using a Fluoromax-4 quantum yield measurement system (HORIBA, Jobin Yvon Inc) connecting an integrating sphere.

Generally, 2 g of graphite powder was put into an 80 solution of concentrated H2SO4 (12 mL) and HNO3 (8 mL). The mixture was kept at 80 °C for 8 h using oil-bath. After cooling to room temperature, the mixture was diluted with 200 mL of DI water. Then, the mixture was washed and centrifuged for several times with DI water to reach neutral. The product was dried under ambient condition overnight. Then the pretreated graphite powder (1.0 g), NaNO3 (1.03 g), and concentrated H2SO4 (100 mL) were placed in a flask. Then, 4.5 g of KMnO4 was slowly added under stirring and the temperature was kept below 20 °C for 2 h. After further vigorous stirring for 24 h at 40 °C, DI water (200 mL) with a speed of 10 mL/min was added into the flask to terminate the reaction and then 3 mL H2O2 (30%) was added to remove the residual oxidant. The mixture was washed by repeated centrifugation with HCl and DI water for several times. Then, GO was dialyzed using membrane (MWCO: 3500) for 5–7 days to remove salts byproduct and excessive acid. And finally, GO powders were obtained by freeze-drying the aqueous GO solution using a lyophilizer.

In a typical preparation process, GO (20 mg) and KO2 (200 mg) were dispersed in deionized water (10 mL). The suspension was stirred for 30 min and then transferred into 20 mL autoclave, sealed and heated at 200 °C for 24 h. After the resulted suspension being cooled and neutralized with HCl, dialyzed (MWCO: 3500) for 3 days, and filtered (membrane filter with 0.22 μm pore size). Finally, freeze-drying was applied to evaporate water and the purified GQDs can be obtained as a yellow solid powder. In addition, GQDs that prepared with other amounts of KO2 (i.e. 100, 500, and 1000 mg) were performed using the same conditions and treatment procedures as above. The preparation of GQDs in KOH was carried out as well as using the same reaction conditions just by replacing KO2 with KOH (158 mg, i.e. an equivalent amount of 200 mg KO2).

In vitro cytotoxicity studies of these materials were carried out with MCF-7 cells by standard MTT (3-(4,5-dimethylthiazol-2-yl)-2,5-diphenyltetrazolium bromide) assay to delineate biocompatibility of the obtained GQDs. Typically, cells were seeded in a 96-well plate with a density of 1 × 104 cells per well and allowed to adhere overnight. Five replicate wells were used for each control and tested concentrations. After cultured in a 5% CO2 incubator at 37 °C for 24 h, cells were treated with the other 100 μL dispersion of GQDs (GQDs were dispersed in Dulbecco's Modified Eagle's Medium (DMEM) with concentrations of 25–100 μg/mL) for another 24 h. And then, 10 μL of MTT (5.0 mg/mL in PBS) was added into each well. After additional 4 h incubation, the growth medium was discarded and 100 μL of DMSO was added into each well to dissolve the colored formazan. Finally, the optical density of each sample was recorded by a microplate reader (Imark 168–1130, Biorad, USA) at a wavelength of 550 nm.

The potential for cellular imaging of the obtained GQDs was tested by use of MCF-7 cells. Firstly, 2.0 mL of MCF-7 cells in DMEM medium with an initial density of 4 × 104 cells per mL were loaded onto glass coverslips and cultured in 5% CO2 incubator for 24 h at 37 °C. The live cells were then incubated with GQDs dispersion (100 μg/mL in DMEM medium) for 8 h at 37 °C. After that, the cells were fixed by 4% paraformaldehyde for 30 min. The samples were rinsed by clean PBS solution twice, and finally were observed with a confocal laser fluorescence microscopy (TCS SP5II, Leica, Germany).

Fig. 1 a shows a typical transmission electron microscopy (TEM) image of the obtained GQDs, and their average diameter is then determined to be of ca. 4.8 nm according to the size distribution plot (Fig. 1b). In addition, a well-resolved lattice fringe with spacing of 0.24 nm can be observed from the high-resolution TEM (HR-TEM) of the GQDs (Fig. 1c), corresponding to the d1120 hexagonal lattice plane of graphite [23]. Topographic morphology of the GQDs show a height profile mainly within a narrow range of 1–2 nm (Fig. 1d), which is similar to the original GO (Fig. S1, ESI) and indicates that most of the GQDs consist of 2–4 layers of GO. Raman spectroscopy was also used to characterize the GQDs (Fig. S2, ESI). The peaks centred at 1350 cm−1 and 1577 cm−1 are attributed to the D and G bands of carbon materials, respectively, confirming the existence of graphitic structure in GQDs. The relative intensity of the D band to G band (ID/IG) for the GQDs is 1.07, which is higher than that of the original GO (0.92), indicating more “disorders” produced when the GO was cut into GQDs [26].

X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS) measurement was carried out to determine the chemical composition of the obtained GQDs. XPS survey spectrum indicates that the GQDs mainly contain carbon and oxygen elements, which is consistent with the original GO (Fig. 2 a). Besides, both of the HR XPS C1s spectra of the original GO and GQDs can be deconvoluted into three peaks locating at about 284.8, 286.8 and 288.5 eV (Fig. 2b and c), corresponding to the bonds of CC/CC, COH/COC and CO, respectively [15,25,27,28]. Significantly, the peak intensity at 286.8 eV (corresponding to epoxy) was greatly reduced from the GO to the GQDs. This observation indicates that the epoxy groups on GO were broken during the hydrothermal reaction process. These XPS results are further confirmed by the Fourier transform infrared (FT-IR) characterization (Fig. 2d). For instance, the peaks at 3400, 1730, 1623 and 1050 cm−1 are assigned to the stretching vibrations of OH, CO from carboxyl, CC, and CO from epoxy, respectively [27,28]. These FT-IR signals demonstrate that similar functional groups are present on the GO and GQDs. Moreover, the obviously reduced peak intensities at 1050 and 1730 cm−1 from GO to GQDs reveal that most of the epoxy and carboxyl functional groups on GO being removed after the hydrothermal reaction.

Subsequently, the optical properties of the as-prepared GQDs are examined. The aqueous solution of GQDs exhibits a board emission centred at 550 nm (Fig. 3 a), and accordingly, which shows bright yellow PL emission (under irradiation of 365 nm UV lamp, Fig. 3a, inset). Such a yellow emission for GQDs has rarely been observed before. The PL excitation spectrum of the GQDs (at λ max = 550 nm) displays two bands with the maxima at about 310 nm and 450 nm (Fig. 3b, red line). The PL QY of the GQDs was measured to be 8.9% in aqueous solution using an absolute QY measurement system. Similarly to other methods for GQDs preparation, the as-obtained GQDs also show broad absorption band ranging from 200 to 500 nm (Fig. 3b, black line). The strong UV absorption below 300 nm is generally attributed to the π→π* transition of CC bond contained in sp [2] domains, while the absorptions tailed from 300 to 500 nm are usually considered to be due to the n→π* transitions of oxygen-containing groups [29]. The photostability testing exhibits that 76% of PL intensity of the GQDs was reserved after irradiation with 150 W Xeon-lamp for 120 min, but only 24% remained for fluorescein isothiocyanate (FITC) (a widely used fluorescent dyes for bio-labelling) (Fig. S3, ESI), indicating much higher photostability of the GQDs than that of FITC.

To further understand optical properties of the GQDs, their PL lifetime was measured using the time-correlated single photon counting (TCSPC) technique. As shown in Fig. 3c, the PL decay curve of GQDs can be fitted with a bi-exponential function (lifetimes to be of 1.85 ns (39%) and 5.64 ns (61%), respectively, and with an intensity weighted average lifetime of 3.16 ns). This bi-exponential decay behaviour of the lifetime suggests the presence of two emissive sites, which are usually attributed to the sp2 domains contained in GQDs and oxygen-containing group at the edge of GQDs [10,30]. Moreover, the influence of pH on the PL of GQDs was also investigated. As shown in Fig. 3d, the PL intensity of GQDs (at λ max = 550 nm) is observed increasing from pH 2 to 9, and then decreasing at higher values (up to pH 12). Interestingly, the PL intensity of GQDs is found linearly proportional to pH values in the range of 2–9, suggesting a potential application as a pH probe. Based on the above XPS and FT-IR analysis, abundant carboxyl and hydroxyl functional groups are confirmed existing at the edge of the obtained GQDs. Therefore, the PL quenching of GQDs under acidic conditions is proposed to be due to protonation of oxygen-containing functional groups. To verify this assumption, PL lifetime of the GQDs in acidic solution was measured. As shown in Fig. S4 and Table S1 (ESI), the decay curve and fitting results of the GQDs in acidic solution are almost the same as that of in neutral condition, this suggesting a static quenching mechanism in acidic solution that could be attributed to the result of protonation [31].

It's known that KO2 can be decomposing into KOH and O2 in water (superoxide anion being the intermediate), a series of control experiments were conducted to clarify the effects of superoxide anion (KO2) and base (KOH) during the preparation of GQDs. First, effects of dosage of KO2 were investigated (the same reaction conditions were applied except different amounts of KO2 being added, see Experimental Section for details in ESI). In the case of 100 mg KO2 being added, some black precipitate was still observed after the reaction, indicating that the “scissors” are not sufficient to cut all the GO into GQDs (a conversion rate being estimated to be ∼20 wt%). When the dosage of KO2 increased to 200 mg, the produced reaction solution became fully transparent and without any precipitate could be observed, implying that GO was cut into GQDs completely. And consequently, a high conversion rate (∼35 wt%) was achieved. Notably, the conversion rate from GO to the GQDs would decrease if the dosage of KO2 were further increased (e.g. 500 mg and 1.0 g), demonstrating that “excessive” KO2 could partly destroy the GO or GQDs. Next, cutting GO in KOH (equivalent amount as that of 200 mg KO2) solution was studied, and large amounts of black precipitate was observed after the hydrothermal treatment. Although the supernatant exhibits weak fluorescence, the PL spectra are observed closely alike with those obtained by using KO2 (Fig. 3a and Fig. S5a in ESI). This finding indicates that the strong base (i.e. OH−) could assist cutting GO sheets into yellow emissive GQDs during the hydrothermal reaction process, though the efficiency is very low (<1 wt%). It has been reported that epoxy groups tend to form along a line on the carbon lattice during the synthesis of GO [32]. The cooperative alignment induces a strong tension, and therefore, the underlying CC bonds trend to break. Considering the FT-IR and XPS C1s spectra of the GQDs displaying less epoxy signal than that of GO, the cutting of GO by KO2 is supposed to be due to the cooperation of superoxide anion and OH−, i.e. the strongly oxidative superoxide anion creating linear epoxy groups on the GO, and then OH− behaving as “scissors” to cut GO into GQDs along these epoxies. And such a process can be simply described in Fig. 4 . Moreover, the PL spectra of GQDs synthesized using different dosages of KO2 are shown in Fig. S5b-d (ESI). As can be seen therein, the GQDs showed similar PL spectra when 100 mg and 200 mg of KO2 were applied. But, when the dosages of KO2 were increased to 500 mg and 1.0 g, the λ max of PL emission gradually blue-shifts from ∼550 nm to ∼500 nm. To further understand this phenomenon, FT-IR spectra of the GQDs prepared using different amounts of KO2 were measured. As shown in Fig. S6 (ESI), the peak at 1050 cm−1 (arising from epoxy's COC) gradually diminished, but the peak at 1710 cm−1 (corresponding to CO) raised when the dosage of KO2 increased from 100 mg to 1.0 g. These results suggest that excessive KO2 could further oxidize hydroxyl (−OH) on the edge of GQDs into carbonyl groups (CO).

With the assistance of KO2, a highly efficient synthesis method of GQDs was developed and the obtained GQDs displayed rarely observed yellow emissive PL properties. Based on the studies described above, several clues can help us to explore the origin of the yellow emission of GQDs. Firstly, both of the UV–vis absorption and PL decay curve of GQDs are highly related to the sp2 domain and oxygen-containing groups of GQDs. In addition, the PL quenching of GQDs under acidic conditions indicate that the oxygen-containing groups played an important role on the PL emission of the obtained GQDs. Therefore, we can conclude that the yellow emission of the obtained GQDs was originated from the cooperation of the sp2 domains contained in GQDs and oxygen-containing group at the edge of GQDs. Moreover, with the increment of KO2 dosage, hydroxyl (−OH) on the edge of GQDs were further oxidized into carbonyl groups (CO), the PL of obtained GQDs were blue-shifted gradually, which further proved that the effects of the edge functional groups on the PL properties of the yellow emissive GQDs. The electron withdrawing effects of carbonyl groups can widen the band gap of the GQDs, thus the PL of the GQDs blue-shifted when the dosage of KO2 increased.

Since GQDs had been commonly demonstrated to be low cytotoxicity and excellent biocompatibility, the as-prepared GQDs in this study are also expected to be employed as PL probe for bio-imaging. To illustrate such an application, the cytotoxicity of the GQDs was firstly evaluated using the standard MTT (3-(4,5-dimethylthiazol-2-yl)-2,5- diphenyltetrazolium bromide) assay against MCF-7 cells as an example. As shown in Fig. 5 a, more than 80% cell viabilities are observed with 25–100 μg/mL of the GQDs being added during incubation of cells, revealing their low cytotoxicity and good biocompatibility. Then, the bioimaging capabilities of the GQDs are preliminary evaluated through cellular imaging experiments. As shown in Fig. 5b–d, the GQDs are found to be able to enter into cells and display intense PL emission, indicating that the as-prepared yellow emissive GQDs could be potentially applied as a bioimaging agent.

In summary, a facile and high-efficient (∼35 wt% conversion rate) approach has been developed for the preparation of GQDs from GO using the commonly hydrothermal method with assistance of KO2. The as-obtained GQDs show numerous superior features including good water-dispersibility, rarely observed yellow PL emission, respectable PL QY yield (8.9%), and low cytotoxicity. Moreover, the GQDs are also observed to show a linear PL intensity with pH (from 2 to 9) and excellent cellular imaging capability, demonstrating their potential applications as a pH probe and bioimaging agent.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This work was supported by the Natural Science Foundation of China (21277149), Zhejiang Provincial Natural Science Foundation of China (LR13B050001), and the Ningbo Science and Technology Bureau (2014B82010).

ce:section.ce:para: The following is the supplementary data related to this article:

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data related to this article can be found at https://doi.org/10.1016/j.carbon.2017.09.011.
